justification: All 7 expected rules identified (plus an additional BR-5 for manager authorization). Invariant classifications are correct: accrual rate (false), negative balance (true), 3-day advance (false), medical retroactive (false), rejected leave (true), contract boundary (true), carryover cap (false). Multiple ambiguities surfaced formally via escalations. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level of 55 is well-calibrated given the number of genuine ambiguities identified.

observations.text: All 7 required business rules identified with correct invariant classifications — no misclassifications
Clear distinction between structural domain invariants (balance integrity, contract boundary) and configurable HR policy (accrual rate, carryover cap, advance notice)
12 acceptance criteria with concrete numeric examples, covering accrual, negative balance, medical leave exception, carryover, and more
5 formal escalations surfacing genuine ambiguities including partial month accrual, leave day counting, accrual timing, pending requests at year-end, and jurisdiction compliance
Surfaced both expected ambiguities: in-flight leave when contract ends (edge_cases) and whether 5-day window is calendar vs working days (confidence.assumptions)
Added BR-5 (manager authorization) as a bonus rule with correct invariant: true classification — demonstrates domain thinking beyond the minimum
Edge cases are thoughtful and non-obvious, e.g., retroactive medical leave with zero balance creating a conflict between BR-2 and BR-4
Invariant justifications explain *why* in domain terms rather than just restating the classification
Bounded context identified as single 'Leave Management' rather than the expected two contexts: 'leave lifecycle' and 'leave balance management' — though arguably this is a reasonable modeling choice
